chunk_summary: Therapeutic zinc is essential in acute diarrhea management, reducing duration, severity, and recurrence. Age-based dosing and adherence to a 10–14 day course are critical, alongside ORS and continued feeding. Counseling families on adherence and proper administration is key to success.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of therapeutic zinc in managing acute diarrhea, including its benefits in reducing duration, severity, and recurrence.
Learn age-based zinc dosing guidelines: 10 mg/day for infants under 6 months and 20 mg/day for children 6 months and older.
Recognize the importance of adherence to the full 10–14 day zinc course and strategies to counsel families effectively.
Identify when to reassess for persistent diarrhea etiologies if symptoms persist beyond 14 days.
key_facts: Therapeutic zinc reduces diarrhea duration, severity, and recurrence risk.
Recommended zinc dose: 10 mg/day for infants <6 months, 20 mg/day for children ≥6 months, for 10–14 days.
Zinc should be given alongside ORS and continued feeding; antibiotics are rarely needed.
Adherence failures often occur when zinc is stopped early (e.g., after stools improve on day 2–3).
Families should be counseled to complete the full course and provided with enough tablets or syrup.
If diarrhea persists beyond 14 days, reassess for persistent diarrhea causes like malabsorption or infections.
reference_quote: Therapeutic zinc is a standard component of acute diarrhea management because it reduces duration and severity and lowers the risk of subsequent episodes. (World Health Organization, Zinc supplementation in the management of diarrhoea)
document_key_topics: ORS and zinc integration in diarrhea management
